description: Symptom: Interfaces in initializing state cause ISL to be out of sync and therefore VSX is not functional, having only Primary peer LAGs in UP state. Scenario: The issue is applicable to LAG and can be seen on reboot path or checkpoint restore if LAG member(s) belong to LC in down state. Workaround: Remove the member interface(s) belonging to LC in Down State from the LAG Config before doing upgrading to 10.17.xxxx.

description: Symptom: Switch is unable to connect to Central. Scenario: This issue may occur when attempting to onboard switches that are connected to Central. The process connecting to YANG is creating more sockets than expected, leading to overload of the yang-resolverd server and subsequent disconnections. Workaround: Restart yang-resolverd daemon to reestablish the operation of the daemon. Restart hpe-restd daemon to make a clean connection to YANG after restarting the yang-resolverd daemon.

description: Symptom: After upgrading to 10.17.0001, LAG member interfaces remain stuck in “Initializing” state. Scenario: This issue occurs during upgrade or reboot on 10.17.0001 when a LAG contains one or more member ports on a line card that is down. Workaround: Before upgrade/reboot, remove from the LAG any member ports that belong to the down line card (or bring that line card online). If already impacted, remove the down-LC member ports from the LAG; the remaining members come up and the LAG forms.

description: Symptom: On VSF supported devices, when LPA Max IP Exemptions configurations are restored from external server, some of the Exempted LPA IPs are not loaded in Internal Cache but are present in show running (OVSDB). ARP/NS requests are not flooded for the missing exempted IPs. Instead they are proxied by the local switch (DUT). Scenario: This issue occurs when Max LPA IP Exemptions (per port or across system) configurations are restored from the external server. Workaround: Unconfigure and reconfigure the impacted IPs using ip local-proxy-arp exclude <ip-address> or ipv6 local-proxy-nd exclude <ipv6-address>. Restart the NDMD daemon and reboot the switch.

description: Symptom: When macsec selftest is executed on 25G MACsec uplink ports, macsec selftest is stuck in "Initialized" instead of "Passed". Scenario: This issue can occur when macsec selftest is executed on 25G MACsec uplink using the following steps: 1. On demand macsec selftest execution 2. Empty XCVR bay / 10G XCVR inserted in 25G MACsec uplink ports. Workaround: For on demand MACsec selftest to pass, do not use an empty port bay or 10G XCVRs. Alternatively, when an "Initialized" state is encountered, perform no macsec selftest to recover the port and reperform macsec selftest. Trigger MACsec selftest on boot: configuring macsec selftest and writing to memory followed by a reboot.

description: Symptom: On 4100/6100 VSF stacks, disabling/enabling a mirror session configured with a GRE tunnel destination to a VRF causes the standby member to enter a boot loop. Scenario: This issue occurs when the mirror session uses destination tunnel <IP> source <IP> vrf <VRF> and the source interface is any interface/LAG member on a non-conductor VSF member (such as, interface 1/1/1 or LAG that includes a non-conductor port). Toggling the mirror session (disable/enable) triggers the reboot of the standby. Workaround: Aoid tunnel VRF destinations in mirror sessions on VSF when the source includes non-conductor ports; use a physical interface destination instead or limit sources to the conductor member. If already impacted or to prevent recurrence: remove the interface from the mirror source, remove the non-conductor member port from the LAG, or delete the mirror session/remove the tunnel destination. Reboot prevention after a trigger is not possible; manual remediation is required to recover from the loop.

description: Symptom: Control plane ACL with an ip-option qualifier drops ICMP packets even when those packets do not carry IP options; pings to the SVI/gateway fail and the deny rule’s hit counter increments. Scenario: This issue occurs when applying a control plane IPv4 ACL that includes a deny matching ip-option any above a permit for ICMP, then ping the VLAN interface address. Workaround: Add an explicit permit rule for the intended traffic (for example, permit icmp any any) placed above the ip-option deny entry, or temporarily remove/relax the ip-option rule until the fixed build is deployed.

description: Symptom: Switch is unable to connect to Central. Scenario: This issue may occur when attempting to onboard switches that are connected to Central. The process connecting to YANG is creating more sockets than expected, leading to overload of the yang-resolverd server and subsequent disconnections. Workaround: Restart yang-resolverd daemon to reestablish the operation of the daemon. Restart hpe-restd daemon to make a clean connection to YANG after restarting the yang-resolverd daemon.

description: Symptom: After upgrading to 10.17.0001, LAG member interfaces remain stuck in “Initializing” state. Scenario: This issue occurs during upgrade or reboot on 10.17.0001 when a LAG contains one or more member ports on a line card that is down. Workaround: Before upgrade/reboot, remove from the LAG any member ports that belong to the down line card (or bring that line card online). If already impacted, remove the down-LC member ports from the LAG; the remaining members come up and the LAG forms.

description: Symptom: On VSF supported devices, when LPA Max IP Exemptions configurations are restored from external server, some of the Exempted LPA IPs are not loaded in Internal Cache but are present in show running (OVSDB). ARP/NS requests are not flooded for the missing exempted IPs. Instead they are proxied by the local switch (DUT). Scenario: This issue occurs when Max LPA IP Exemptions (per port or across system) configurations are restored from the external server. Workaround: Unconfigure and reconfigure the impacted IPs using ip local-proxy-arp exclude <ip-address> or ipv6 local-proxy-nd exclude <ipv6-address>. Restart the NDMD daemon and reboot the switch.

description: Symptom: The DHCP lease start and end timestamps are not being recorded in the Client Insight per VLAN table. Scenario: When a client obtains an IP address from the DHCP server while DHCP Snooping is enabled, the DHCP lease information is expected to be captured and stored in the Client Insights OVSDB tables. However, the system is currently not logging the DHCP RENEW start and end times in the relevant OVSDB tables, even though the client successfully receives the DHCP lease.

description: Symptom: When macsec selftest is executed on 25G MACsec uplink ports, macsec selftest is stuck in "Initialized" instead of "Passed". Scenario: This issue can occur when macsec selftest is executed on 25G MACsec uplink using the following steps: 1. On demand macsec selftest execution 2. Empty XCVR bay / 10G XCVR inserted in 25G MACsec uplink ports. Workaround: For on demand MACsec selftest to pass, do not use an empty port bay or 10G XCVRs. Alternatively, when an "Initialized" state is encountered, perform no macsec selftest to recover the port and reperform macsec selftest. Trigger MACsec selftest on boot: configuring macsec selftest and writing to memory followed by a reboot.

description: Symptom: On 4100/6100 VSF stacks, disabling/enabling a mirror session configured with a GRE tunnel destination to a VRF causes the standby member to enter a boot loop. Scenario: This issue occurs when the mirror session uses destination tunnel <IP> source <IP> vrf <VRF> and the source interface is any interface/LAG member on a non-conductor VSF member (such as, interface 1/1/1 or LAG that includes a non-conductor port). Toggling the mirror session (disable/enable) triggers the reboot of the standby. Workaround: Aoid tunnel VRF destinations in mirror sessions on VSF when the source includes non-conductor ports; use a physical interface destination instead or limit sources to the conductor member. If already impacted or to prevent recurrence: remove the interface from the mirror source, remove the non-conductor member port from the LAG, or delete the mirror session/remove the tunnel destination. Reboot prevention after a trigger is not possible; manual remediation is required to recover from the loop.

description: Symptom: Control plane ACL with an ip-option qualifier drops ICMP packets even when those packets do not carry IP options; pings to the SVI/gateway fail and the deny rule’s hit counter increments. Scenario: This issue occurs when applying a control plane IPv4 ACL that includes a deny matching ip-option any above a permit for ICMP, then ping the VLAN interface address. Workaround: Add an explicit permit rule for the intended traffic (for example, permit icmp any any) placed above the ip-option deny entry, or temporarily remove/relax the ip-option rule until the fixed build is deployed.
